stories.headline: Hungary's Orbán and Magyar Hold Final Rallies Ahead of Sunday Election
stories.summary:
Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orbán and opposition leader Péter Magyar held their final campaign rallies Saturday, one day before a national election that could end Orbán's 16-year grip on power. Magyar, a 45-year-old lawyer and former Fidesz insider, addressed thousands in Debrecen — traditionally a Fidesz stronghold — calling the vote a day of "resurrection" and pledging national reconciliation if he wins. Most independent polls show his center-right Tisza party leading by double digits.

Orbán closed his campaign on Budapest's Castle Hill, warning supporters that "we are in an age of danger" and framing the election around external threats, particularly the war in neighboring Ukraine. He urged voters not to take risks with inexperienced leadership, a central message of his campaign.

Many analysts caution that the final result may be closer than polls suggest, noting Fidesz's historically strong ability to mobilize rural voters. If Magyar's Tisza party prevails, it would mark one of the most consequential political shifts in Central Europe in years, with significant implications for Hungary's relationships with the European Union and NATO.

stories.headline: Kings Shut Out Oilers 1-0 as Panarin Scores in Playoff Push
stories.summary:
The Los Angeles Kings defeated the Edmonton Oilers 1-0 Saturday, with Artemi Panarin scoring on a first-period breakaway and Anton Forsberg making 27 saves for his third shutout of the season. The win was the Kings' fourth straight and significantly strengthened their playoff positioning in the Western Conference, pulling them to within two points of Vegas and Anaheim for third place in the Pacific Division.

Forsberg's performance appeared to bolster his case over incumbent starter Darcy Kuemper heading into the postseason. The Kings held NHL scoring leader Connor McDavid in check throughout, and he was called for two minor penalties, including a critical holding infraction late in the game.

Despite the loss, Edmonton later clinched its seventh consecutive postseason berth when Winnipeg lost to Philadelphia. The Oilers have eliminated the Kings in each of the last four playoffs, adding additional significance to Saturday's result as both teams may be set for another postseason meeting.

stories.headline: Red Wings Eliminated from NHL Playoff Contention with Loss to Devils
stories.summary:
The Detroit Red Wings were eliminated from NHL playoff contention Saturday after a 5-3 loss to the New Jersey Devils, extending the franchise's postseason drought to ten consecutive seasons — the league's longest active streak. Jesper Bratt scored the go-ahead goal with 3:34 remaining in the third period, and Dawson Mercer added an empty-net goal to seal the result.

Detroit had held a playoff position for 148 days during the season, raising hopes among fans in a hockey-passionate market that had not seen postseason play since 2016. The Red Wings took leads in each of the three periods but surrendered all three, a pattern that encapsulated their inconsistency down the stretch.

Jack Hughes scored for New Jersey and added an assist, continuing his strong season. The Devils' win secured their own playoff positioning heading into the final days of the regular season.

stories.headline: Machete Attack at Grand Central Station Leaves Three Injured; Police Kill Suspect
stories.summary:
A man wielding a machete attacked three people randomly at the 42nd Street-Grand Central subway station in New York City on Saturday morning before being shot and killed by police. Officers responded to an emergency call at approximately 9:40 a.m. and encountered the suspect, identified as Anthony Griffin, 44, who was behaving erratically and claiming to be "Lucifer."

Police Commissioner Jessica Tisch said officers ordered Griffin to drop his weapon 20 times and attempted to de-escalate the situation before an officer shot him twice as he advanced toward them with the knife extended. Griffin was pronounced dead at Bellevue Hospital. He had three prior unsealed arrests.

The three victims — an 84-year-old man, a 65-year-old man, and a 70-year-old woman — suffered injuries not considered life-threatening, though one man sustained significant lacerations to the head and face along with an open skull fracture. Chief of Transit Joseph Gulotta said the attacks appeared to be random.

New York Governor Kathy Hochul expressed gratitude to officers for acting quickly to stop the suspect and said state authorities were working closely with the NYPD as the investigation continued. The subway station, one of the busiest in the city, was temporarily closed as police investigated the scene.

stories.headline: U.S. Intelligence Indicates China Sending Shoulder-Fired Missiles to Iran Amid Ongoing Conflict
stories.summary:
American intelligence officials have assessed that China may have recently shipped shoulder-fired missiles to Iran for use in its ongoing conflict with the United States and Israel, according to reporting from the New York Times. The development, if confirmed, would mark a significant escalation of Chinese involvement in the conflict and a direct challenge to U.S. military operations in the region.

The disclosure comes as U.S. and Iranian officials are engaged in historic direct peace talks in Islamabad, with negotiations continuing late into the night on Friday, according to Iranian state media and a White House official. The timing of the intelligence report adds pressure to an already fragile diplomatic process, raising questions about whether Beijing is attempting to strengthen Tehran's hand at the negotiating table.

China has publicly called for a diplomatic resolution to the U.S.-Iran conflict while maintaining close economic and strategic ties with Tehran. Washington has repeatedly warned Beijing against providing material support to Iran, and this reported weapons transfer, if verified, could trigger fresh diplomatic confrontations between the world's two largest economies.

The revelation also complicates the broader geopolitical landscape surrounding the Hormuz crisis, with roughly 3,200 vessels backed up outside the strait and European fuel shortages deepening. Analysts say Chinese weapons support for Iran could harden the positions of both hawks and doves in the Islamabad negotiations, potentially threatening the fragile ceasefire framework.

stories.headline: Navy Warships Enter Strait of Hormuz for Mine-Clearing Operations, Pentagon Says
stories.summary:
U.S. Navy warships have crossed into the Strait of Hormuz to conduct mine-clearing operations, the Pentagon announced Friday, marking a significant military maneuver as ceasefire talks between American and Iranian officials continue in Islamabad. The operation is aimed at reopening the critical waterway, which has been effectively closed to commercial shipping during the conflict, causing widespread disruption to global energy markets.

The mine-clearing mission carries considerable risk, as the strait remains a contested zone and any incident could quickly escalate tensions at a moment when diplomats are working to hold a fragile ceasefire together. The United States has framed the operation as necessary to restore freedom of navigation and alleviate the economic pressure building on allies and global markets.

Iran has not immediately commented on the naval operation. Tehran has previously warned that any military activity in what it considers its territorial waters would be viewed as a provocation. The Hormuz closure has pushed energy prices to record levels and prompted European airports to warn of fuel shortages.

The simultaneous pursuit of military operations and diplomatic negotiations reflects the complex balancing act the U.S. faces in the region. Supporters of the administration argue the mine-clearing mission demonstrates resolve and is essential to economic relief; critics caution that military actions in the strait risk derailing the Islamabad talks at a critical juncture.

stories.headline: Hundreds of Irish Petrol Stations Run Dry as Fuel Protests Enter Fifth Day
stories.summary:
Hundreds of petrol stations across the Republic of Ireland have run out of fuel as protests and blockades of fuel distribution infrastructure entered their fifth consecutive day on Friday, the BBC reported. The disruptions have created significant shortages for motorists and businesses across the country, with queues forming at stations that remain operational.

The protests are linked to broader economic grievances, including the impact of surging global energy prices driven by the Hormuz crisis. Irish hauliers and farming groups have been among those participating in blockades, arguing that fuel costs have become unsustainable for their industries and that the government has been slow to respond with relief measures.

Irish authorities have been monitoring the situation closely, with the government under mounting pressure to negotiate with protesters or deploy emergency fuel reserves. The crisis underscores how the U.S.-Iran conflict and the Hormuz closure are producing cascading economic consequences far beyond the Middle East, affecting everyday citizens across Europe.

Ireland's situation is part of a wider pattern of fuel stress across the continent. European airports have already flagged potential aviation fuel shortages, and energy analysts warn that the longer the Hormuz strait remains closed to normal commercial shipping, the more severe downstream supply disruptions will become across Western economies.

stories.headline: Pope Leo XIV Condemns 'Delusion of Omnipotence' Driving U.S.-Israel War in Iran, Demands Negotiations
stories.summary:
Pope Leo XIV on Saturday issued his strongest condemnation yet of the ongoing U.S.-Israel military conflict with Iran, denouncing what he called the 'delusion of omnipotence' fueling the war. The newly installed pontiff demanded that political leaders halt hostilities and enter into peace negotiations, marking a significant escalation in the Vatican's public stance on the conflict.

The statement represents a notable moment for Pope Leo XIV, who has moved quickly to assert the Catholic Church's moral authority on global security matters since his election. The Vatican has historically sought to position itself as a neutral mediator in international conflicts, and this direct rebuke of the United States and Israel signals an unusually assertive posture from the new pope.

The remarks come as direct U.S.-Iran talks are underway in Islamabad and as Israeli strikes in Lebanon continue to draw international criticism. The pope's call for negotiations aligns with pressure from a growing number of world leaders urging a diplomatic resolution, though Washington and Jerusalem have defended their military actions as necessary to counter Iranian nuclear ambitions and regional aggression.

No immediate response from the White House or Israeli government to the pope's statement had been issued at the time of reporting. The intervention adds a significant moral and diplomatic voice to an already complex international situation, with the Vatican's influence potentially affecting Catholic-majority nations' positions on the conflict.

stories.headline: Historic U.S.-Iran Direct Talks Begin in Islamabad as Fragile Ceasefire Holds
stories.summary:
Face-to-face negotiations between the United States and Iran got underway Saturday in Islamabad, Pakistan, marking the highest-level direct engagement between the two countries since the 1979 Islamic Revolution. Vice President Vance is leading the American delegation as sources confirm trilateral talks are now actively under way, with Pakistan serving as host and intermediary.

Details of the negotiations remain limited, but the talks come amid a fragile ceasefire following weeks of conflict that disrupted global energy markets and caused significant casualties. The stakes are substantial: a successful agreement could reopen the Strait of Hormuz, where more than 3,200 vessels remain backed up, and ease oil prices that have driven a surge in U.S. inflation.

Iran had previously declared U.S. ceasefire terms 'unreasonable,' and the outcome of the Islamabad talks remains deeply uncertain. Analysts describe the summit as a 'make-or-break' moment, with Pakistan's stated goal being the modest one of simply keeping dialogue alive rather than reaching a comprehensive settlement.

For Vance, the negotiations carry significant domestic political weight as well, requiring him to balance competing factions within the Trump administration, Congressional pressure, and his own political future. Protests linked to high consumer prices caused by the war have already been reported in several U.S. cities.

stories.headline: Trump Administration Admits Significant Error in New York Medicaid Fraud Probe Figures
stories.summary:
The Trump administration this week acknowledged it made a major factual error in statistics used to justify a federal fraud investigation into New York's Medicaid program, admitting first to the Associated Press that figures cited by Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services Administrator Dr. Mehmet Oz were drastically inflated. Oz had claimed that roughly 5 million New Yorkers received personal care services through Medicaid last year — nearly three-quarters of all enrollees — calling the figure 'unheard of' and demanding the state 'come clean.'

The actual number was approximately 450,000, or between 6% and 7% of total enrollees, CMS spokesman Chris Krepich told the AP. He said the agency had misidentified New York's approach to applying billing codes and has since refined its methodology, though the probe remains ongoing.

Health policy analysts said the error raised broader questions about the evidentiary basis for the administration's sweeping anti-fraud campaigns across the country, many of which have targeted Democratic-led states. 'These numbers could have been cleared up in a phone call, so it's really slapdash,' said Fiscal Policy Institute senior health policy adviser Michael Kinnucan.

The administration maintained that despite the error, it still has substantive concerns about New York's oversight of personal care services and is reviewing the state's formal response. Supporters of the anti-fraud effort argue that regardless of the specific figure, Medicaid waste and abuse remain legitimate targets for federal oversight.

stories.headline: BTS Launches First World Tour in Nearly Four Years Following Members' Military Service
stories.summary:
K-pop supergroup BTS opened their long-awaited 'ARIRANG' world tour Thursday night at Goyang Stadium near Seoul, drawing tens of thousands of fans for the group's first headline tour performance since their 2021-22 Permission to Dance on Stage series. All seven members — RM, Jin, Suga, j-hope, Jimin, V, and Jung Kook — returned to the stage after completing South Korea's mandatory military service, with Suga being the last discharged in June 2025.

Despite heavy rain, the stadium, which holds more than 40,000 people, was packed with fans who had traveled from as far as Russia, the United States, and Brazil. More than a hundred additional fans who could not obtain tickets stood outside with umbrellas to listen to the performance. The set drew from both the group's catalog and their new fifth studio album, also titled 'ARIRANG.'

The South Korean concerts, running through Sunday, launch a global tour spanning dozens of shows across North America, Europe, and Asia. Industry analysts estimate the tour could generate hundreds of millions of dollars in revenue per quarter, underscoring BTS's continued commercial dominance nearly a decade after their international breakthrough.

The tour comes less than a month after the group held a free comeback concert at Seoul's Gwanghwamun Square. For many fans, the reunion carried deep personal significance; one attendee said anticipating the concert helped her endure a year-long battle with cancer.

stories.headline: Netanyahu Orders Direct Lebanon Negotiations as Israel-Lebanon Border Clashes Continue Despite Broader Ceasefire
stories.summary:
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has ordered officials to begin direct negotiations with Lebanon "as soon as possible," citing requests from Beirut, even as Israeli and Hezbollah forces continue exchanging fire along the southern Lebanese border. Lebanon declared a day of mourning on Saturday after Israeli strikes killed at least 200 people and wounded more than 1,000 in a single day, with the toll representing one of the deadliest single-day episodes of the conflict.

Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian condemned the Israeli strikes as a violation of the U.S.-Iran ceasefire framework, warning that continued attacks threatened to collapse the diplomatic process underway in Islamabad. Tehran stated it would not abandon Lebanon, signaling that Israeli operations against Hezbollah remain a major obstacle to broader de-escalation.

U.S. President Donald Trump said American forces would remain deployed in the region around Iran until a "real agreement" is fully enforced, warning of further conflict if terms are not met. UN Secretary-General's envoy Jean Arnault met with Iran's Deputy Foreign Minister Kazem Gharibabadi in Tehran and visited strike-damaged sites, with further talks expected in Pakistan.

The situation underscores the fragility of the ceasefire architecture, with Israeli military operations against Hezbollah continuing in parallel with U.S.-Iran diplomatic engagement. Critics of the Israeli strikes argue they risk unraveling weeks of diplomatic progress, while Israeli officials maintain that neutralizing Hezbollah's military capacity is a prerequisite for any durable regional peace.

stories.headline: Meta Must Face Massachusetts Lawsuit Alleging Instagram Designed to Addict Children
stories.summary:
Massachusetts' top court ruled Friday that Meta Platforms must face a lawsuit brought by the state's attorney general alleging the company deliberately designed its Instagram platform to addict children. The ruling allows the case to proceed, marking a significant legal development in ongoing state-level efforts to hold social media companies accountable for harms to minors.

The Massachusetts attorney general's office argues that Instagram's features — including algorithmic content feeds, notifications, and engagement mechanics — were knowingly engineered to maximize compulsive use among young users. Meta has denied that its platforms are designed to harm children, arguing it has invested heavily in safety tools and parental controls.

The ruling is one of several legal fronts Meta faces across the country over youth mental health and platform addiction claims. Dozens of states and school districts have filed similar suits, and Congress has held repeated hearings on the issue. The Massachusetts court's decision to allow the case to move forward could embolden other jurisdictions and increase pressure on the company to settle or change its practices.

Meta's stock and broader Big Tech shares have been closely watched as legal liability around youth safety grows. The company has pointed to features such as default time limits for users under 16 and restrictions on certain content as evidence of good-faith efforts to protect young people on its platforms.

stories.headline: UniCredit Abandons Russian Unit Sale, Considers Liquidating Banking License
stories.summary:
Italian banking giant UniCredit has scrapped its plans to sell its Russian subsidiary and is now weighing liquidating the business and surrendering its banking license in Russia, according to a report Friday by the Russian financial daily Kommersant, citing unnamed sources. The move marks a significant shift in strategy for one of Europe's largest banks, which has faced sustained pressure from European regulators and investors to exit Russia following the country's 2022 invasion of Ukraine.

UniCredit had previously attempted to find a buyer for its Russian operations but reportedly struggled to complete a sale amid regulatory hurdles, Western sanctions, and Russian government restrictions on asset transfers by foreign companies. Liquidation would represent a full wind-down of the bank's Russian presence rather than a transfer of ownership.

The potential exit adds to a broader trend of European financial institutions pulling back from Russia, though many have found the process far more complicated and costly than anticipated due to Kremlin-imposed conditions on foreign asset disposals. UniCredit has previously disclosed significant exposure to Russia on its balance sheet, drawing criticism from shareholders and European Central Bank supervisors.

UniCredit has not officially confirmed the report. The bank's CEO has previously said the institution was working toward an orderly exit while managing its legal and financial obligations. The final decision on liquidation versus sale has not been publicly announced.

stories.headline: Russian Drone Strikes Kill at Least Two in Odesa Residential Area
stories.summary:
Russian drone strikes struck a residential area of the Ukrainian port city of Odesa overnight Saturday, killing at least two people and wounding two others, according to officials. The attack targeted civilian infrastructure in one of Ukraine's most strategically important southern cities.

Ukrainian emergency services responded to the scene, with rescue workers searching through damaged buildings. Odesa has been a recurring target of Russian drone and missile attacks given its role as a critical hub for Ukrainian grain and commercial shipping exports on the Black Sea.

The strikes come as international diplomatic efforts around the broader Russia-Ukraine conflict continue, with Ukraine maintaining that Russian attacks on civilian areas undermine any prospects for negotiated settlement. Kyiv has repeatedly called on Western allies for additional air defense systems to protect population centers from drone barrages.

Russia has not officially commented on the specific overnight strikes. Moscow has consistently characterized its military operations as targeting military infrastructure, a claim Ukrainian authorities and Western governments dispute.

stories.headline: Generative AI Outperforms Average Human on Creativity Tests in Landmark Study
stories.summary:
A major new study comparing more than 100,000 people with today's leading generative artificial intelligence systems found that AI can now surpass the average human on certain standardized creativity tests, according to results published Friday. The research marks one of the most comprehensive head-to-head comparisons of human and machine creative performance to date.

The study tested participants and AI systems on divergent thinking tasks — measures of the ability to generate original, varied, and unexpected ideas — which are widely used in psychology as proxies for creative cognition. The AI models tested consistently scored above the human average on these benchmarks, though researchers noted that the highest-performing human participants still exceeded the AI systems at the top end of the distribution.

Scientists cautioned that performance on standardized creativity tests does not capture the full range of human creative experience, including emotional depth, cultural context, and intentional meaning-making. The findings nonetheless reignited debate among researchers and technologists about the nature of machine intelligence and the long-term implications for creative professions.

The results are likely to fuel ongoing policy and academic discussions about AI's role in education, the arts, and the workforce. Proponents argue the findings highlight AI's potential as a creative collaborator, while critics warn against overstating the significance of benchmark scores that may not translate to genuine originality or artistic value.

stories.headline: FAA and Pentagon Approve Counter-Drone Lasers for Use Along Southern Border
stories.summary:
The Federal Aviation Administration and the Defense Department have signed a joint agreement authorizing the use of counter-drone laser systems along the U.S.-Mexico border, concluding that the weapons can be deployed safely without necessitating airport closures. The announcement follows a review prompted by airspace disruptions near El Paso earlier this year, when lasers were used without proper FAA notification, stranding travelers.

The FAA's decision came after a demonstration at White Sands Missile Range in New Mexico last month convinced regulators that the lasers pose an acceptable risk to civilian aviation. The agreement outlines safety precautions but has not publicly detailed their specifics, and the FAA has yet to hold a classified briefing for Congress on the matter.

Drones are a persistent threat along the border, with officials reporting more than 27,000 drone detections within 1,600 feet of the southern border in the final six months of 2024 alone. The Trump administration has framed the laser program as essential to countering Mexican cartel surveillance and drug-smuggling operations.

Some Democratic lawmakers expressed cautious support while pressing for more transparency. Rep. Rick Larsen, ranking Democrat on the House Transportation and Infrastructure Committee, said meaningful interagency collaboration was "absolutely critical" and that the FAA must be present whenever counter-drone systems are deployed in ways that could affect national airspace safety.

stories.headline: Shohei Ohtani Surpasses Ichiro Suzuki with 44-Game On-Base Streak
stories.summary:
Los Angeles Dodgers star Shohei Ohtani broke a record held by Hall of Famer Ichiro Suzuki on Friday night, reaching base safely in his 44th consecutive game with a fifth-inning single against the Texas Rangers. The streak is the longest ever recorded by a Japanese-born player in Major League Baseball history, surpassing Suzuki's 43-game run with the Seattle Mariners in 2009.

Ohtani's streak began on Aug. 24 of last season and carried through the final 31 games before continuing into all 13 of his appearances this season. Dodgers manager Dave Roberts noted that Ohtani has yet to fully hit his stride offensively, suggesting the streak may still have room to grow.

The milestone marks the second time Ohtani has surpassed Suzuki in a major statistical category. In 2024, Ohtani broke Suzuki's record for stolen bases by a Japanese-born player in a single season, finishing with 59 steals. Ohtani also received an intentional walk in the eighth inning Friday, a sign of the continued respect opposing teams show him at the plate.

stories.headline: Celtics Tie NBA Record with 29 Three-Pointers, Clinch Eastern Conference No. 2 Seed
stories.summary:
The Boston Celtics tied the NBA and franchise record for three-pointers made in a single game Friday night, draining 29 from beyond the arc in a 144-118 blowout victory over the New Orleans Pelicans. The win clinched the Eastern Conference's No. 2 playoff seed and the Atlantic Division title for Boston.

Sam Hauser led all scorers with 24 points on eight three-pointers, while Jaylen Brown added 23 points in just 29 minutes before sitting out the fourth quarter. Payton Pritchard contributed 21 points and 10 assists, and eight different Celtics players hit at least two three-pointers. Star forward Jayson Tatum sat out to rest following his return from Achilles tendon surgery.

The Celtics' 29-three-pointer total matches their output from Oct. 22, 2024 against the Knicks, and equals records also held by the Memphis Grizzlies and Milwaukee Bucks. New Orleans, missing multiple starters including Zion Williamson, has now lost nine of its last 10 games and will miss the playoffs for the second consecutive year.

stories.headline: Trump Administration Unveils Design for 250-Foot 'Triumphal Arch' in Washington
stories.summary:
The Trump administration has unveiled architectural plans for a 250-foot 'Triumphal Arch' to be constructed in Washington, D.C., a monument that would stand taller than both the U.S. Capitol building and the Lincoln Memorial if approved. The design was announced publicly on Friday and immediately drew attention for its scale and symbolic ambition.

Few additional details about the project's location, cost, or timeline have been released. The proposal would require approval from relevant federal authorities before construction could proceed. The announcement adds to a series of high-profile aesthetic and infrastructure initiatives put forward by the administration.

The proposal has drawn comparisons to monumental architecture in European capitals and has prompted early debate about its appropriateness, cost to taxpayers, and the message it would send about the character of American civic life. Critics and supporters alike are expected to weigh in as further details emerge.

stories.headline: Israel's Beirut Strikes Kill Hundreds, Threatening U.S.-Iran Ceasefire
stories.summary:
Israeli airstrikes on densely populated areas of Beirut killed more than 300 people in a single day, according to the Lebanese Ministry of Health, bringing the total death toll from Israel's Lebanon campaign to nearly 2,000. Rescue workers searched rubble overnight as civilians described scenes of widespread destruction with no warning before the strikes. The assault represents a sharp intensification of Israel's campaign, which it says targets Tehran-backed Hezbollah militants.

The strikes have thrown the fragile U.S.-Iran ceasefire into serious jeopardy. Iran and Pakistani mediators insist Lebanon was explicitly included in the truce, while confusion persists over the agreement's actual scope. Iranian parliamentary Speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf warned that continued attacks on Lebanon would bring 'explicit costs and strong responses.'

International condemnation was swift and broad. British Foreign Secretary Yvette Cooper called the escalation 'deeply damaging,' EU foreign policy chief Kaja Kallas said it was 'hard to argue' the strikes were carried out in self-defense, and French Foreign Minister Jean-Noël Barrot called them 'intolerable.' President Trump reportedly called Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu to urge a scaling back of the strikes.

The Beirut bombardment adds a volatile new dimension to the Islamabad negotiations led by Vice President JD Vance, where the U.S. and Iran are attempting to reach a broader settlement. Critics of Israeli military conduct argue the strikes were indiscriminate, hitting civilian residential areas; Israel maintains the operations target Hezbollah infrastructure and personnel.

stories.headline: Vance Warns Iran Not to 'Play' the U.S. as Islamabad Negotiations Begin
stories.summary:
Vice President JD Vance departed for Islamabad on Friday to lead U.S.-Iran talks aimed at ending the six-week-old conflict, delivering a pointed warning before boarding Air Force Two: 'If they're gonna try and play us, then they're gonna find that the negotiating team is not that receptive.' Vance said he believed negotiations could be 'positive' if Iran engages in good faith, framing the U.S. position as offering an 'open hand' while setting firm limits on tolerance for bad-faith tactics.

The talks come as the temporary ceasefire appears close to collapse. The gap between U.S. and Israeli demands and Iran's public positions has been described as wide, and Iran's parliament speaker Mohammad Bagher Qalibaf issued pointed warnings tied to Israel's ongoing strikes in Lebanon, which Iranian officials say violate the ceasefire terms.

Trump has assigned Vance — long known for skepticism toward foreign military entanglements — to find a resolution, amid mounting domestic political and economic pressure fueled by surging energy costs. The move is seen as both a diplomatic signal and a political calculation, given Vance's potential 2028 presidential ambitions.

Supporters of the administration's approach argue that a strong negotiating posture is necessary to prevent Iran from stalling while continuing to consolidate leverage. Critics, including some Democrats, contend that Trump's earlier threat to destroy Iran's 'whole civilization' has complicated diplomacy and hardened Iranian public opinion against concessions.

stories.headline: Iran's New Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei Suffering Severe Facial and Leg Injuries, Sources Say
stories.summary:
Iran's new supreme leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, is recovering from severe and disfiguring facial and leg injuries sustained in the Israeli airstrike that killed his father, Ali Khamenei, at the outset of the conflict, according to three sources close to his inner circle who spoke to Reuters. The extent of his injuries had not been previously reported publicly.

The revelation adds a significant new dimension to understanding the internal dynamics of Iran's leadership during the ongoing conflict and ceasefire negotiations. Mojtaba Khamenei assumed the supreme leadership position following his father's death in the early weeks of the war, and questions about his physical condition and decision-making capacity could have implications for the Islamabad talks.

Iran has maintained tight control over information regarding its senior leadership throughout the conflict. The sources' willingness to speak to Reuters about the injuries suggests some degree of internal uncertainty or deliberate signaling, though the motivations remain unclear.

The disclosure comes as Vice President Vance arrives in Islamabad for direct negotiations, raising questions about whether Iran's leadership is in a position to make and sustain major strategic decisions under the current circumstances.

stories.headline: Melania Trump Delivers Rare White House Statement Denying Jeffrey Epstein Ties
stories.summary:
First lady Melania Trump delivered an unusual public statement at the White House on Thursday, denying any connection to convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein and calling accusations linking her to him 'completely false' and 'financially and politically motivated.' Reading from a prepared text in the Grand Foyer, she called on Congress to hold public hearings at which Epstein's survivors could testify and have their accounts entered into the congressional record.

The statement was described as extraordinary given the rarity of Melania Trump's public appearances and its timing, coming as the Epstein controversy had largely faded from headlines amid the Iran conflict. West Wing officials confirmed they were aware she planned to make a statement, though the White House press office did not clarify whether the content was known in advance. She left without taking questions.

Two of Epstein's accusers, Maria and Annie Farmer, responded by saying they sought 'accountability, transparency, and justice.' Former Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene called the statement 'brave,' while Democrats criticized it as likely to reignite scrutiny of the administration's handling of Epstein-related matters. President Trump had previously urged the media to move on from the story.

The statement immediately returned the Epstein case to the political spotlight. Critics questioned why the first lady chose to address the matter publicly at this moment, while supporters argued she had a right to defend her reputation. The White House did not offer additional elaboration on the circumstances surrounding the statement.

stories.headline: Kamala Harris Says She Is Considering a 2028 Presidential Run
stories.summary:
Former Vice President Kamala Harris said Friday she is considering another run for the presidency in 2028, publicly floating the possibility for the first time since her defeat in the 2024 election. The comments were made as former Transportation Secretary Pete Buttigieg also suggested he may launch a presidential campaign, signaling an early start to what could be a competitive Democratic primary field.

Harris's potential candidacy would make her a leading figure in a party currently without a dominant frontrunner for 2028. Her 2024 campaign ended in a loss to Donald Trump after she entered the race late following President Biden's withdrawal. Supporters argue she brings national name recognition and an established donor network; critics within the party contend her 2024 performance exposed structural weaknesses that a second campaign would need to overcome.

The Democratic primary landscape remains fluid, with several figures — including Buttigieg and others — signaling interest in the race. Harris's statement stops short of a formal declaration, and her team has offered no timeline for a final decision.

The remarks drew immediate attention given the party's ongoing debate about its direction and messaging in the Trump era. Some Democrats welcomed the prospect of Harris running again, while others argued the party needs fresh faces and a new approach to recapture the White House in 2028.
